overview.description: Seeking problem-solving assistance and difficult relationships in an engineering department of a large manufacturing firm in the aerospace industry.

data_collection.details: The firm delivers specialised designs and produces complex, technical components as part of an international supply chain. Its engineering department comprises 11 cross-functional units, each with its own formal leader, plus a general leadership unit; the engineers engage in a variety of complex tasks in design engineering and analysis.

nodes.node_exclusion: Non-respondents were not included in the network (all of them were of low hierarchical rank).

ties.label: problem_solving
ties.name_generator: When you need to engage in creative problem-solving regarding your job, who are the people you go to, to help you think outside of the box and consider different aspects of the problem innovatively?

ties.label: difficult
ties.name_generator: Who do you find difficult to work with?

additional_information: The data setting was chosen as an exemplar of a highly knowledge-intensive workplace: aerospace manufacturers construct complex, heterogeneous systems and rely on continuous innovation. The original filled protocol (Brennecke 2020, AMJ) classified 'difficult relationships' under 'Affective' at the top level; here we follow Borgatti et al. (2009) and encode it as 'social relations / difficult relationships (affective)'. Country, region, city, and data-collection dates are not reported in the article and are recorded as 'unknown'.
